title: Rewrite Raft Consensus content: hook, intuition, WHY steps, real-world
description:
Current teaching grade: F (8/36). Raft opens with a dry definition, has no analogy, step descriptions say WHAT not WHY, and no real-world applications are mentioned.

New content to implement:

HOOK: 'Five servers need to agree on the order of operations — even when servers crash and networks split. Without consensus, your bank could show two different account balances. Raft is how Kubernetes and CockroachDB solve this.'

INTUITION: 'Think of Raft like a classroom. The teacher (leader) tells everyone what to write down. If the teacher is absent, students vote on a substitute. The substitute only becomes the new teacher if the majority agrees.'

SAMPLE WHY STEPS:
1. 'Node-0's timer expired — it hasn't heard from a leader, so it starts an election.'
2. 'Node-0 needs votes from a MAJORITY (3/5) to prevent two leaders — the pigeonhole principle.'
3. 'Node-0 got 3 votes → becomes leader → immediately sends heartbeats.'

REAL-WORLD: etcd (Kubernetes), CockroachDB, TiKV, Consul

SUMMARY: Leader handles writes → replicates to followers → majority required for election AND commit → new election in 150-300ms if leader dies
acceptanceCriteria: Description replaced with hook + intuition (not definition-first)
At least 5 event types include WHY explanation in step descriptions
Properties panel includes 'Used By' section with 4+ real systems
3-bullet memorable summary visible in properties panel
Complexity intuition added: O(n) messages per election, why Raft scales to 3-7 nodes

title: Rewrite Consistent Hashing content: show the PROBLEM before the solution
description:
Current teaching grade: F (5/36). No intuition, no problem statement, no progression.

New content:

HOOK: 'When you add a server to your cache cluster, how many entries need to move? With regular hashing: almost ALL. With consistent hashing: only 1/N. This one idea is why Cassandra can add servers without downtime.'

INTUITION: 'Imagine servers at positions around a clock. Data walks clockwise until it finds a server — that's its home. Add a new server, and only nearby data moves.'

PROGRESSION: Add 1 node + 100 keys → all go to one node. Add 2nd node → ~50 move. Add 3rd → ~33 move. Show 1/N redistribution vs modular hashing's ~67% redistribution.

REAL-WORLD: Cassandra, DynamoDB, memcached, CDN routing

SUMMARY: Ring + clockwise lookup → adding a node moves only 1/N keys → virtual nodes fix imbalance
acceptanceCriteria: Description starts with the PROBLEM (modular hashing fails on scaling)
Intuition analogy present before any technical detail
Properties panel includes 'Used By' with 4+ real systems
Guided progression: 'First add one node... now add a second...' prompts
Complexity info: O(log n) lookup, O(K/n) redistribution

title: Rewrite Gossip Protocol content: use the gossip/rumor analogy
description:
Current teaching grade: F (6/36). The concept is literally NAMED after gossip/rumors but the content uses 'epidemic-style information dissemination' as its description. Massive missed opportunity.

HOOK: 'How does information spread through a school? One person tells two friends, who each tell two friends... Gossip protocols use this exact principle to sync thousands of servers.'

INTUITION: 'Each server picks a random friend and shares what it knows. Within log(N) rounds, everyone knows everything — just like how rumors spread exponentially.'

REAL-WORLD: Cassandra (membership + failure detection), Consul (SWIM protocol), DynamoDB

SUMMARY: Random peer selection → version-based push → O(log N) convergence → no single point of failure
acceptanceCriteria: Description uses rumor/gossip analogy
Step descriptions explain WHY each gossip exchange happens
Convergence explained: 'Watch the convergence bar — exponential spread'
Properties panel includes real-world usage
O(log N) convergence mentioned with intuitive explanation

title: Rewrite CAP Theorem content: debunk 'pick 2' myth, lead with partition inevitability
description:
Current teaching grade: D (13/36). The SEO content correctly mentions PACELC but the simulation description presents the standard 'C, A, P tradeoffs' without the critical clarification.

HOOK: 'Partitions WILL happen. Your network WILL split. The question is: when it does, do you sacrifice consistency or availability?'

MISTAKE TO DEBUNK: 'You do NOT choose 2 of 3 like a menu. Partition tolerance is mandatory.'

REAL-WORLD: CP = HBase, MongoDB (strict), Zookeeper. AP = Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB.

SUMMARY: Partitions are inevitable → choose C or A during partition → PACELC adds latency tradeoff during normal operation
acceptanceCriteria: Description starts with partition inevitability, not 'CAP states that...'
Properties panel explicitly debunks 'pick 2 of 3' myth
Real databases classified: CP list and AP list
PACELC mentioned with 'during normal operation' latency tradeoff

title: Rewrite Paxos content: explain WHY two phases are needed, add Raft comparison
description:
Current teaching grade: F (5/36). Paxos is the hardest concept in the module and has the worst teaching score.

HOOK: 'If Raft is like a classroom with a teacher, Paxos is like a committee deciding by vote — without a permanent chairperson.'

INTUITION: 'Phase 1: Before proposing, check if anyone else already proposed. Phase 2: Now propose your value.'

WHY TWO PHASES: 'Because two proposers could propose simultaneously and get conflicting acceptances. Phase 1 establishes priority.'

COMPARISON: 'Raft = always has a leader → simpler. Paxos = leaderless → more resilient but harder.'
acceptanceCriteria: Description includes intuition for WHY each phase exists
Properties panel includes 'Why Two Phases?' explanation
Comparison with Raft in properties panel
Real-world usage: Chubby, Spanner, Megastore

title: Rewrite remaining 6 concepts: 2PC, Saga, MapReduce, Vector Clocks, Lamport, CRDTs
description:
All remaining concepts scored F or D. Apply the same content improvement pattern: hook (problem-first), intuition (analogy), WHY steps, real-world applications, memorable summary.

2PC HOOK: 'Booking a flight AND hotel together. If the hotel is full, cancel the flight too.'
SAGA HOOK: 'Each service does its part — if something fails, undo everything in reverse.'
MAPREDUCE HOOK: 'Google needed to process the entire internet. No single computer could do it.'
VECTOR CLOCKS HOOK: 'No global clock in distributed systems. How do you know which event happened first?'
LAMPORT HOOK: 'If event A happened before B on different servers, how do you know?'
CRDTs HOOK: 'Three people edit the same document offline — and when they reconnect, all edits merge without conflicts. That's how Figma works.'
acceptanceCriteria: All 6 concepts have problem-first hooks
All 6 have intuition analogies
All 6 have at least 3 WHY step descriptions
All 6 have 'Used By' real-world section
All 6 have 3-bullet memorable summaries

title: Add 'Predict & Verify' self-test prompts to all simulations
description:
T11 (Self-Test) scored 0 across ALL 11 concepts. No simulation asks the user to predict what will happen before showing it.

For each simulation, add at least 2 prediction prompts:
- Raft: 'Which node will win the election?'
- Consistent Hashing: 'How many keys will move?'
- CAP: 'Will this write succeed in CP mode?'
- Gossip: 'How many nodes will have the data?'
- CRDTs: 'What will Replica A's value be after merge?'
- 2PC: 'P2 voted NO — Commit or Abort?'
- Paxos: 'Whose value will win?'
acceptanceCriteria: At least 2 prediction prompts per simulation (22 total minimum)
Prompts appear BEFORE the action
After the action, a brief 'Did you predict correctly?' note appears
User can dismiss/skip predictions

title: Add common mistakes / misconceptions for top 5 concepts
description:
T8 (Common Mistakes) scored 0 across ALL 11 concepts.

Mistakes to add:
- CAP: 'MYTH: You choose 2 of 3. REALITY: Partitions are inevitable; you choose C or A.'
- Raft: 'MISTAKE: Heartbeats are special. REALITY: Heartbeats are empty AppendEntries RPCs.'
- Consistent Hashing: 'MISTAKE: More nodes = better distribution. REALITY: Virtual nodes matter more.'
- CRDTs: 'MISTAKE: All CRDTs handle all operations. REALITY: G-Counter can only increment.'
- 2PC: 'MISTAKE: 2PC guarantees success. REALITY: 2PC is BLOCKING — coordinator crash leaves participants stuck.'
acceptanceCriteria: Top 5 concepts have 'Common Mistakes' expandable section
Each mistake has MYTH/REALITY format
At least 2 mistakes per concept

title: Add Raft vs Paxos interactive side-by-side comparison
description: T7 (Comparison) scored 0 for both Raft and Paxos. The module has BOTH algorithms but no way to compare them. Add a comparison mode where the same failure scenario runs on both simultaneously.
acceptanceCriteria: Same failure scenario runs on both protocols
Side-by-side or sequential comparison view
Summary table: leader-based vs leaderless, message complexity, failure handling
Properties panel explains when to choose each

title: Add Guided Tour mode for Raft (Secret Lives of Data style)
description: Secret Lives of Data's Raft explanation is universally praised because it's a GUIDED NARRATIVE. Our simulation dumps everything at once. Add a 'Guided Tour' button that walks through: (1) What are nodes and why consensus (2) How election works (3) How log replication works (4) What happens when leader crashes.
acceptanceCriteria: Guided Tour button visible on Raft simulation
4-step narrative: intro → election → replication → failure
Each step auto-configures the simulation
Narration text explains WHY at each step
User can exit tour at any point
